For reference, [Bazel rules_go naming for fields on targets related to compile/link options](https://github.com/bazelbuild/rules_go/blob/master/docs/go/core/rules.md#go_binary): 
- `copts` == CGO_CFLAGS
- `cppopts` == CGO_CPPFLAGS
- `cxxopts` == CGO_CXXFLAGS
- `clinkopts` == CGO_LDFLAGS

So there is some precedent in not using `cflags` / `ldflags` naming. Although I'm not particularly in favor of `clinkopts` as any better than `ldflags`. Going verbose here and having it be similar to the `cc` backend's naming choices will lead to a more consistent experience for Pants users.

Instead of the final template, the snippet (App Insights resources) is added into the working template. 

This change is similar to the [X-Ray Tracing change](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/pull/3817). We considered options like the current choice (adding an option in the init flow to add or not add the resources) and adding the snippet in init templates. We decided to go with the current approach because it's not flexible to allow enable/no-enable in the init templates repo. Also, if we made the change in init templates instead, we would have to modify each template to have one version with the added resources and one version without.

The key difference is that an IntEnum behaves like an int.
> IntEnum is the same as Enum, but its members are also integers and can be used anywhere that an integer can be used. If any integer operation is performed with an IntEnum member, the resulting value loses its enumeration status.

This enables you (mostly) to transparently use them in a context where you are working with integers, but also want what enums offer, like here. :-)

This is testing some internal cache code that attempts to reduce loads. If I started by loading ref_doy, then padding would also load ref_doy-1, and ref_doy+1. If I then load ref_doy+1, the cache will recognize it already has ref_doy, and ref_doy+1, and only needs to load ref_doy+2. If a load is outside the cache window, then the cache needs to be reset with a full 3 day load. Here I'm making sure that the cache recognizes it needs to reset itself and load data around the new load date. If the cache didn't do that, the left padded data would be from 11 days previous.

The problem is that `std::map` explicitly sorts and `std::unsorted_map` is an unstable hashmap, like Python before version 3.6, so we can't fix it by just replacing the `std::map`. I looked for another `std::map` subclass that maintains insertion order, but didn't find any. The answers to [this StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/q/1098175/1623645) suggest work-arounds (rather like the one I used above). In the case above, the `std::map` is just used to communicate to pybind11 that we want the output to be a `py::dict`. I could have filled a `py::dict` directly in src/python/forms.cpp, and that would solve the issue for C++/Python interface in new Pythons, but not internal C++ usages.

We need to maintain a table associating the "service instance name" with the current TXT data and port number and possibly associated "host name" and importantly the associated the address(es) of the host. This may be needed to detect changes (e.g. new address on host, change to TXT data) in addition to removal of a service instance. For example, if there is a change in TXT data of an existing service instance, it would be better for platform to call `otPlatTrelHandleDiscoveredPeerInfo()` first with old TXT data and `mRemoved` set to remove old TXT data before calling it again with new TXT data. This ensures that old TREL Peer table entry is removed properly and not kept in the table unnecessarily.

The question is where should we maintain this (in the platform or add it in as part of core). In my view, it makes sense to delegate this to platform implementation since it is more related to how the underlying DNS-SD library works. 

So the trel platform APIs are designed such that they only expect the information that they care about a peer (i.e. TXT record and socket address). They don't get the "instance name" (or "host name") since practically they have no use for them.

```
class Task(CCIDictModel):

2. A full validation of the merged configuration, so that we can catch missing required fields -- without needing to write custom validators.

```
# Exists interface named Foo with a single field named id
type Bar implements Foo {
  name: String
  id: String
}
```

then it should be legal to rename the `name` field, just like if `Bar` implemented no interfaces. I've benchmarked with [this script](https://gist.github.com/pmeier/6bf44e22f29f0bc840f8b9ba6a7f3830), which does the following:

1. Load one annotations json, i.e. one annotation type like instances or captions, from a zip archive.
2. Group all annotations of the same image id together.
3. Match the grouped annotations with the corresponding meta information of the image.

These steps are performed once without datapipes and once with. This is the result:

```
without datapipes 9.711 seconds
with datapipes 520.147 seconds

The current pattern to register a factory is to use a global module variable. I followed it in this PR but I think we should move away from it because:
- unless every module that defines a subclass of SQLAlchemyObjectType explicitly calls `register_connection_field_factory`, you may not get the expected connection when you import a `SQLAlchemyObjectType` subclass. 
- it is not possible to use different factories within the same schema.

Instead I would prefer to explicitly define the factory on the `Meta` class of `SQLAlchemyObjectType`:
```python
class MyType(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
     class Meta:
         connection_field_factory = ...
         ...
```

This behavior could easily be shared within an app by putting this in an abstract meta class. Let me know if you agree with this change and I'll update this PR.

Adding a formatter interface sounds reasonable to me, but this doesn’t
really feel like the right structure. The `ExperimentMetadataField`
struct is tightly coupled to the two implementations of formatters. The
`readable_name` nad `formatter` fields are only relevant to the
`ReadableFormatter`, and the `json_key` field is only relevant to the
`JsonFormatter`. And as written, although the name `Experiment*` is
everywhere, nothing actually knows about experiments.

Why introduce a new type with a big list of vague “fields” when we
already have the experiment proto? The interface could just be

```python
class ExperimentFormatter(metaclass=abc.ABCMeta):
    def format(self, experiment):
        """Format an experiment.

        Args:
          experiment: An `experiment_pb2.Experiment` value.

        Returns:
          A string.
        """
        pass
```

The `ReadableExperimentFormatter` would encapsulate the readable field
names and how to format them, and the `JsonExperiment` would encapsulate
the JSON keys and how to order and indent them. The tests would be on
the same output that we would actually see from the binary rather than
just an arbitrary set of fields defined in the test case.

Then, you could remove the giant block of field definitions in
`uploader_main.py`, too: just `print(formatter.format(experiment))`.

Because it breaks things :-).

Stacks normally add their own link-local IPv6 address, though they may have options to avoid doing so -- but unfortunately, some stacks (mac OS in particular) don't.

When we get notified that the address has been added (as is happening here), openthread will not allow you to add this (second) link-local address to its list of addresses (I believe openthread rejects all addition of link-local addresses, as they are internally handled).  

So if we do NOT remove it, then the stack will have an address that openthread doesn't know about.

But worse than that, the stack will create packets with that address as a source address, and send them down to openthread for sending onto the network.  Other nodes will attempt to respond, but the openthread stack will not "receive" these packets because they're not bound for a known address.

A few thoughts on this:
* This seems rather deeply nested. I'd like to avoid getting so nested, especially in this function. It hurts readability. I also don't think we need to do it this way.
* Rather than crawling every combination of segments and comparing parents, I don't think we need to get that intense about it. Rather, I think a simpler and more straight-forward logic here would be to do this:
    1. Check if there's a "/" in the command path. If not, then the prefix to use is `''` (all strings technically start with `''`). If there IS, do `prefix, _ = command_path.rsplit('/' 1)` to get the prefix. 
    2. Filter the valid paths for those that start with the prefix. You can either use `filter()` or a list comprehension. 

Done this way, it would look more like:

```suggestion
        if '/' in self.context.command_path:
            prefix, _ = self.context.command_path.rsplit('/', 1)
        else:
            prefix = ''
        all_paths = self._valid_stack_paths() 
        # We'll fall back to all_paths if no valid path starts with the prefix
        filtered_valid_stack_paths = [p for p in all_paths if p.startswith(prefix + '/')] or all_paths
        if len(filtered_valid_stack_paths) > MAX_VALID_STACK_PATH_COUNT:
            filtered_valid_stack_paths = []
```

After our offline discussion I thought about the possibilities that we have and decided to go with a keyword argument parameter. In my opinion we retain maximum flexibility with this approach while ensuring forwards compatibility:

- Arguments can easily be added without breaking existing code
- Arguments can be deprecated and raise a warning if they are used easily
- No state in callbacks is retained, child classes can define their own constructor without having to call `super()`
- Clear documentation of keyword arguments is easier than documentation of class attributes

Hi @jamiedemaria 
As I mentioned in Slack about the performance, when I was testing my implementation of static and multi-dimensional partitions, I notice a huge performance drop (from 5 seconds materialisation to 5 minutes and more). I tracked it down to this line and using `DELETE FROM` statement which apparently becomes significant when we deal with large number of rows. In our case, this is the case where we deal with static partitions. Cutting long story short, I could regain the performance by bypassing the direct `DELETE` through creating an new table with rows which we want to keep, dropping the original table, and then recreating the original table from the temporary table, and finally dropping the temporary table. My `_get_cleanup_statement` and `_get_where_clause` functions look like below:
```
def _get_cleanup_statement(table_slice: TableSlice) -> str:
    """
    Returns a SQL statement that deletes data in the given table to make way for the output data
    being written.
    """
    if table_slice.partition:
        where = _get_where_clause(table_slice.partition, delete=True)
        st0 = """DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temporary;"""
        st1 = f"""CREATE TABLE temporary as select * FROM {table_slice.schema}.{table_slice.table} {where};\n"""
        st2 = f"""DROP TABLE {table_slice.schema}.{table_slice.table};\n"""
        st3 = f"""CREATE TABLE {table_slice.schema}.{table_slice.table} AS SELECT * FROM temporary;\n"""
        st4 = """DROP TABLE temporary;"""
        return st0, st1, st2, st3, st4
        # return (
        #     f"DELETE FROM {table_slice.schema}.{table_slice.table}\n"
        #     + _get_where_clause(table_slice.partition)
        # )
    else:
        return f"DELETE FROM {table_slice.schema}.{table_slice.table}"

def _get_where_clause(table_partition: TablePartition, delete: bool = False) -> str:
    time_partitioned: bool = False
    static_partitioned: bool = False
    multi_partitioned: bool = False
    start_dt_str: str = None
    end_dt_str: str = None
    static_part_str: str = None
    DUCKDB_DATETIME_FORMAT = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"
    log = get_dagster_logger()
    log.info(table_partition)

    if table_partition.time_window:
        start_dt, end_dt = table_partition.time_window
        start_dt_str = start_dt.strftime(DUCKDB_DATETIME_FORMAT)
        end_dt_str = end_dt.strftime(DUCKDB_DATETIME_FORMAT)
        time_window_key = table_partition.time_window_key
        time_partitioned = True

    if table_partition.static_part:
        static_part_str = table_partition.static_part_key
        static_part_curr_key = int(table_partition.static_part_curr_key)
        static_partitioned = True
    
    if static_partitioned and time_partitioned:
        multi_partitioned = True

    if time_partitioned:
        if delete:
            start = f"""WHERE {time_window_key} <= '{start_dt_str}' """
            end = f"""OR {time_window_key} > '{end_dt_str}'"""
            where_clause = start + end
        else:
            start = f"""WHERE {time_window_key} >= '{start_dt_str}' """
            end = f"""AND {time_window_key} < '{end_dt_str}'"""
            where_clause = start + end
    elif static_partitioned:
        if not delete:
            where_clause = f"""WHERE {static_part_str} != {static_part_curr_key}"""
        else:
            where_clause = f"""WHERE {static_part_str} = {static_part_curr_key}"""
    elif multi_partitioned:
        if delete:
            start_col = f"""WHERE {static_part_str} != {static_part_curr_key} """
            start = f"""({time_window_key} <= '{start_dt_str}' """
            end = f"""OR {time_window_key} > '{end_dt_str}') """
            where_clause = start_col + start + end
        else:
            start_col = f"""WHERE {static_part_str} = {static_part_curr_key} """
            start = f"""{time_window_key} >= '{start_dt_str}' """
            end = f"""AND {time_window_key} < '{end_dt_str}' """
            where_clause = start_col + start + end
    else:
        raise ValueError("I just ran out of options!")
    log.info(where_clause)
    return where_clause
```

@vfdev-5 and I discussed offline and our answer is no. As explained in #6517, `fill=None` and `fill=0` is not the same. However, some v1 transforms (the three highlighted here) simply coerce `None` into `0`:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/55d3ba621fc49cc7352167e64bc0cb02bffef9a7/torchvision/transforms/transforms.py#L1338-L1339

AFAIK this behavior is undocumented. In v2 we pass through `None` values:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/55d3ba621fc49cc7352167e64bc0cb02bffef9a7/torchvision/transforms/v2/_utils.py#L56-L61

Note that in both cases, `fill=None` is *not* the default value

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/55d3ba621fc49cc7352167e64bc0cb02bffef9a7/torchvision/transforms/transforms.py#L1321

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/55d3ba621fc49cc7352167e64bc0cb02bffef9a7/torchvision/transforms/v2/_geometry.py#L333

So v1 is silently ignoring user input, while v2 doesn't.

If you have code like this:
```python
my_big_function_that_is_so_long("my_big_string_that_is_so_long")
```
And then black "fixes" it because it's too long, then write it like this:
```python
msg = "my_big_string_that_is_so_long"
my_big_function_that_is_so_long(msg)
```

Now black is happy, and it's more readable too. If you depend on the line length, say by tiling your monitor with windows, you don't want a random line poking off. And if you don't really mean the line length limit, change it - that's one of the (~2?) settings black allows. But I think this promotes better, more readable code (and I like 0 configuration - a so-so standard is better than perfect but non-standard).

Will the library part ever be used by third-party applications not written within the Zephyr project? In that case, we need to at least do the following:

  1. Define the public API of the library (preferably prefixing internal stuff with `_`)

  2. Maintain API compatibility between versions

If the public API is "everything", then it's more of a grab bag of code than a library, and maintaining API compatibility will be extremely hard.

Going on personal experience, maintaining a library used by third parties is a ton more work than just having e.g. a shared codebase. If we don't need a library, we should definitely avoid it.

The previous implementation was a copy once on request solution so by definition it was write through.  It did have the downside that readonly was not enforced thus you could potentially alter the array copy using the double slice operator but it was never reflected in Java so it leaves the impression that work was done that wasn't.

``` 
ja=JArray(JInt)([1,2,3,4)
b = ja[:]
b[1] = 5
print(ja)  <== produces [1,2,3,4]
```

The new implementation writes through because the slice is actually a view.  But it wont permit writing through of the memory view because we can't pin the array.  Hence you may be confused between the writing of the slice view and writing of the byte buffer view than backs it.  We can support writing the slice just fine now, but byte buffer which requires creating a memoryview, accessing the memory, then deleting the view is problematic.

The solution with `klass.__metaclass__.__call__` will however slightly change the legacy behavior. `klass.__metaclass__.__call__` parameters will get patched, instead of `klass.__init__`.

I've found out that there was a difference between parameters going to `klass.__new__` and `klass.__init__`, which actually should be an issue. If your `klass.__new__` was supposed to return another class (let's say `int`), but had `klass.__init__` defined, it would return `int` only when parameters are passed in the code, not in the config. Here's test case describing the current behavior:

```python
@config.configurable
class ConfigurableClassWithNew(object):
  """A configurable class with __new__."""

  def __new__(cls, return_none=False):
    if return_none: # this will test __new__ and __init__ separation
      return None
    return super(ConfigurableClassWithNew, cls).__new__(cls)

  def __init__(self, return_none=False):
    pass

...

  def testConfigurableClassWithNewCanReturnOtherType(self):
    config_str = """
      ConfigurableClassWithNew.return_none = True
    """
    config.parse_config(config_str)
    instance = ConfigurableClassWithNew()
    self.assertIsNotNone(instance)

    config_str = ""
    config.parse_config(config_str)
    instance = ConfigurableClassWithNew(return_none=True)
    self.assertIsNone(instance)
```

After patching `klass.__metaclass__.__call__` in both cases we will have `None` (more what I'd expect). @dhr, @sguada will that change break antyhing for you?

Accepting only strings when deserializing IP fields is something that I can agree on. But achieving this via inheriting from `String` is something that is not so clear for me. From the sources and the discussion I can see two approaches on the `String` base class.

First approach is the one I described in previous comment, where `String` based fields deserialize into `str`, and have specific validator attached. Examples of such fields are `URL` and `Email` which are just string values with additional format constraints. If so, `UUID` filed nor `IP` field should inherit from `String`, since they are not based on a validator and doesn't serialize into string.

The second approach, is the one where `String` based fields are like `String -> Any -> String`. So, if I understand it correctly, all fields that serialize to strings, regardless of deserialized type, should inherit from `String`. Then, `UUID` and `IP` fields should inherit from `String`, as well as all `DateTime` and `Time` fields, which currently inherit from `Field`.

`src/blib2to3` is the source of the data to include and `blib2to3` is the destination path. PyInstaller creates an one-folder bundle that contains an executable so Pyinstaller needs to know where to put the extra data in the bundle directory. `dist` is `blib2to3` and not `src/blib2to3` because PyInstaller hijacks calls to `pkgutil.get_data`. The one file executable PyInstaller can create is really just a bootloader plus an embedded archive that is decompressed and extracted on execution. 

A better explanation is available here: https://pyinstaller.readthedocs.io/en/stable/spec-files.html#adding-data-files

Yes. It is correct that we don't re-schedule the timer in this case but I think it is fine. If the timer happens to be scheduled for the entry that we removed, once it fires we go through and re-schedule it (if needed). 
- In general, I actually slightly favor if we avoid canceling/updating a running timer if we don't need to. We can have a lot of running `Timer` objects in OT stack (they are are added in a linked-list) and removing or re-scheduling one can take more CPU time compared to letting it fire (and then re-scheduling it if we need to when it fires).
- And in some cases it can lead to smaller code size (to be fair, the case here may not be a good example of this but in other situations not canceling a timer may result in smaller code size :)...)

I just realized that we forgot to update docs in #67. This introduced locking mechanisms for batch aggregation and locking operations. Without this there was a possibility to creating an endless loop when aggregator ran too often - if it wouldn't be able to complete the whole operation in one cycle, it would start adding new task messages to Redis and they queue would be growing infinitely, as the tasks from the ongoing run would be duplicated.
So we added locks to prevent simultaneous runs, but if one run can take more than 30 minutes, the `COMPLETION_AGGREGATOR_AGGREGATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` variable should be increased to prevent releasing the lock too early as a result of a timeout. Unfortunately the max duration of a single run will depend on the platform's usage, so it's not possible to provide sane values for everyone here.

Given how frequently we update Pex...would you like a `--pex` flag that is shorthand for `--internal --tool lambdex`? I agree this is titchy.

> Ideally a user could ./pants <update lock goal> -p pex and have what lock files need updating figured out

For a user, that is what they do. When you update a tool lockfile, you can either do `./pants generate-lockfiles` (all) or `./pants generate-lockfiles --resolve=black`. When you update a user requirement, you can either do `./pants generate-lockfiles` or `./pants generate-lockfiles --resolve=my_user_resolve`.

It's a weird situation for pantsbuild/pants because we don't just have our internally used lockfiles; we also need to generate default tool lockfiles. Those default tool lockfiles might not be == the tool lockfile we use internally. Hence the script.

Sorry for the delay in responding.  Considering `get_torch_rocm_macros()`, the exception should occur if and only if the pytorch version is old.  `get_torch_rocm_macros()` is used in two places. 

First, it is used in `is_torch_rocm_v2()` so that the compilation test can use the correct macros for ROCm.  That means `is_torch_rocm_v2()` can fail if either pytorch is old (such that `COMMON_HIPCC_FLAGS` is not present), or pytorch is new enough but was not compiled for ROCm (i.e., THH.h header not present), or some other unknown error has occurred that I can't predict.

`get_torch_rocm_macros()` is used again in the body of `build_torch_extension_v2`.  But this occurs only in the branch where we are compiling horovod with `HOROVOD_GPU=ROCM` and `is_torch_rocm_v2()` are both `True`.

Yes, `get_torch_rocm_macros()` can still succeed if compiling for CUDA when pytorch is sufficiently new.  `get_torch_rocm_macros()` represents a necessary but not sufficient condition for indicating a ROCm build, but otherwise will not affect CUDA.  If compiling for CUDA, the compiler check in `is_torch_rocm_v2()` will definitely fail, regardless of `get_torch_rocm_macros()` succeeding, and set `have_rocm` to `False`.

So I replaced it with a `^` which enforces the pattern match from the beginning of the string.

It is a habit of mine to never leave memory allocated or a list with contents.   Just like Py_CLEAR, if you leave something with a valid point in the destructor and it somehow ends up back in the dtor again (race condition, stack ordering, etc) then the object will delete the memory again.    Now this policy does hide those really evil errors when that condition does come up, but as those are usually transient edge cases and the errors that they cause are almost always going to be of the "heap corruption" type (which burn a week or more to solve), clearing a list on a dying object is usually the safest option.

This isn't the canonical publish artifact for these (pypi), and we don't use them in any workflows. In that sense, this change  exclusively creates more work; it's a thing that can break that we don't use or have tests for, create confusion or distrust (if we break this in the future without noticing and publish different versions to pypi and GH releases).

It also can't be said to be more consistent, as we're not releasing _all_ of the artifacts, just the Python wheels.

We _could_ however use these for publishing, which would guarantee the artifacts are the same _and_ preserve the originals (since GitHub Actions expire eventually). To do this, you'd need to:

1) Update [publish.mjs](https://github.com/finos/perspective/blob/master/tools/perspective-scripts/publish.mjs) to download artifacts from a release instead of a build.
2) Upload all artifacts (including NPM artifacts) to release.

Totally agree, but as I tried to stress out in the PR description, this is not an optimisation, but a refactor that can accommodate for other encoders with size constraint more easily. The ticker is there to have more control over the GIL. One of the benefits of doing partial encoding (as it's currently done) is the guarantee that all threads would get a chance to run while traces are being encoded. The ticker idea in this change makes that promise more reliable by ensuring that threads won't block for too long while a large trace is being encoded. Therefore, there shouldn't be any performance impacts coming from this change (just slightly better response time percentiles at most).

If program can't get the last-created temp directory, then it have to create a new one. There are two solution as I know. The first one is storing the name of temp directory somewhere in the disk, and the second one is using a fixed name. I prefer the second one so I use `gettempdir()+suffix_name` and then `mkdir()`.

> But let's say someone really wants to define a static method in a .rst file by hand (not based on JS code),

That's a good point.

Taking a look at "the way the wind is blowing" in Sphinx, I see `py:staticmethod` was introduced in 0.4 and the `:staticmethod:` option in 2.1. Corresponding top-level directives have not been introduced since then for similar options like `:final:`, `:abstractmethod:`, and `:async:`. I think the wind is blowing in the direction of options, and we should go with it. Also, options allow you to combine prefixes, like `async static final`.

The ideal, then, would be to support just a `:static:` option on `js:function`. Of course, we don't own `js:function`. Here's what I'd recommend:

1. For now, let's merge your patch but not tell anyone about `js:staticmethod`. Let it be a private directive which we'll ultimately aim to obsolete. And let's delete the test that makes sure it's there; the other one will act as a sufficient integration test. (If you can figure out how to add the prefix without having a separate directive, even better.)
2. In Sphinx itself, add a `:staticmethod:` option to `js:method`, where it belongs. Once that merges in, we can start using it.

Looking farther ahead, I think sphinx-js will eventually stop using `js:function` and instead provide its own rendering. That will allow us to do things not dreamt of by Sphinx, like hyperlinking TypeScript type names to their definitions.

It's just because we call `train.report` internally to pipe data from Train -> Tune:

* The first `train.report` is called by the user in their worker code: `train.report(Checkpoint.from_directory(local_dir)`
    * We upload the checkpoint and send `Checkpoint(fs=storage_filesystem, path=".../checkpoint_0000x")` from each worker to the coordinator (trainable).
* The second `train.report` is now called in `DataParallelTrainer`: `train.report(..., Checkpoint(fs=storage_filesystem, path=".../checkpoint_0000x"))`.
    * All workers have sent back this checkpoint, and now we need to report it to Tune for book-keeping.
    * We shouldn't try to upload anything on this 2nd call.

A file has obviously always a set of permissions defined. My sentence here had to be interpreted as "these calls to open are not setting any _specific_ permission".

So for the calls to `open` that was fine on Linux, but not on Windows:
* in `log.py`, it was because `NamedTemporaryFile`, despite the fact that it is claimed to create a temporary file in the most secured manner, is not on Windows. The bare call to `os.open` just let the DACL be inherited from upper level. On Linux, mode is set to 0o700 which is fine.
* in `webroot.py`, it was because `os.umask` is invoked before creating the file. So on Linux this umask is honored and file is created with the correct permissions. But Windows is not using this umask, so we need to use the `filesystem` functions to make this work correctly.

The last situation I anticipated, but it is not encountered in certbot codebase, would be a call to `os.open` with `mode` parameter explicitly set.

When instance_count is a number, what do we assume about it? We map it to `{"*": {"clusterSpec": "initialInstanceCount"}}`. We do not need to worry about the rest of `"*.clusterSpec"`

When instance_count is a dict (e.g., `{"*": 2, "foo": 4}`), we map it to `{"*": {"clusterSpec": {"initialInstanceCount": 2}}, "foo": {"clusterSpec": {"initialInstanceCount": 4}}}`

If the instance-count--based SRD was passed directly to the API without downstream changes, then the call would fail on the API side

So this is where `add_override` can handle adding the instance-count--based SRD to the default SRD

--//--

Alternatively you could start with the default SRD, and add a separate method for overriding based on the instance count

```
class SRD(object):
    @classmethod
    def from_sys_req(...): ...
    def override_instance_count(self, instance_count):
        # Nasty logic
```

In this design, the instance count is treated differently, and would never generate an SRD on its own

The problem with the DEMs is that there's not really a standard format; .hgt & .tiff seem to be the most common, but to handle them properly is best done with a dedicated library like GDAL. However, GDAL is in C and requires a pretty complicated install process that I would hesitate to add to pvlib, even optionally. The actual algorithm for calculating the horizon is overshadowed by the necessary wrapper code to handle everything properly. We've been going back and forth on if that part would be usable in pvlib, or as a separate package, since it ends up as more of a geography package then anything related to PV.

My other concern with that is that the vast majority of the time, I would advise somebody interested in modeling PV performance to just use the pvgis method added here. The only reason I'd foresee actually needing to download the DEM is if you were also modeling uneven terrain, and since you'd already have the DEM you might as well calculate the horizon as well. But all in all, I think adding code to process DEMs to pvlib might not be worth the feature creep/support cost when we can just point people to pvgis anyway.

Yes, because all strings are stored contiguously end-to-end.
If you wanted to replace a single string for example, you could have copied the initial part of the string buffer, then the new string, then the remaining string buffer. But you'd still need to adjust all offsets. 
So basically any string manipulation requires modifying the entire string buffer and offsets buffer.

Which means that doing many small edits on a string column is very expensive. Conceivably, we could create a special type of "EditableStringColumn" which could for example use Rope data structure, or even a simple Gap buffer (the way most text editors are implemented). But that's a non-trivial amount of work.

I changed the code to leave `'request': request` and pass the request to the `Template.render ` function.

I also had a deeper look at the warning, which only popped up, when the user was logged in:

`[...]/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/template/defaulttags.py:63: UserWarning: A {% csrf_token %} was used in a template, but the context did not provide the value.  This is usually caused by not using RequestContext.`

It looks like the contents (e.g. csrf token) of `'request': request` in the custom context is never processed via `django.template.context_processors.request`, because if `request=request` is missing in the `Template.render` function, `Context` instead of `RenderContext` is used in the `make_context` function (called by the `Template.render` function).

Without adding `request=request` to the `Template.render` function, there is no csrf token added to the template (even if there is a `'request': request` in the custom context). But the check, if a csrf token is included in the template tag is only done by `CsrfTokenNode` in`template.defaulttags`, if a user is authenticated und therefore the warning did only appear if a user was logged in.

    * smart contract token address is a differently-versioned hash160 of its txid

    * if the smart contract spends tokens, it appends to the account balances table with a back-pointer to the tx that caused it to spend

```python
def sholl_crossings(neurites, center, radii):
    """Calculate crossings of neurites.

    This function can also be used with a list aa neurites, as follow:

        secs = (sec for sec in nm.iter_sections(neuron) if complex_filter(sec))
        sholl = nm.features.neuronfunc.sholl_crossings(secs,
                                                       center=neuron.soma.center,
                                                       radii=np.arange(0, 1000, 100))

    Args:
        neurites(list): morphology on which to perform Sholl analysis, or list of neurites
        center(Point): center point
        radii(iterable of floats): radii for which crossings will be counted

    Returns:
        Array of same length as radii, with a count of the number of crossings
        for the respective radius
    """
    def _count_crossings(neurite, radius):
        """Used to count_crossings of segments in neurite with radius."""
        r2 = radius ** 2
        count = 0
        for start, end in iter_segments(neurite):
            start_dist2, end_dist2 = (morphmath.point_dist2(center, start),
                                      morphmath.point_dist2(center, end))
            count += int(start_dist2 <= r2 <= end_dist2 or
                         end_dist2 <= r2 <= start_dist2)

        return count

    return np.array([sum(_count_crossings(neurite, r)
                         for neurite in iter_neurites(neurites))
                     for r in radii])
```

Your solution overcome the problem for the tests, but you will still have it outside tests. Because lexicon is used as a library, not only as a cli. And as a library, a developer would expect to create a lexicon client, call the authenticate method, and then execute several actions. So it will fail, because the session will be claused after the first action.

I think it is a design problem. You want to open the session during the authentication phase. You should open the session for each action then close at the end. Like this, each action is independant from each other, and all other problems that are related to the ending session handling that I noted would also vanish. 

The question now is: why do you want to create the session during the authentication phase, and is it a problem to have it open and closed several time if there is several actions (like for instance during a create/update/delete that will need also to call a list at some point).

If it is only a problem of save some requests,I will say this to you: I also tried to do it with ovh, it gave me only problems. Better to have some requests more, less speed, and have a complete isolation between actions.

2. Push batching and formatting into a background thread, with formatted batches pushed into a queue.

> [calling conventions]

Hmm.

I don't think we should allow or expose `PIPE` as a thing we let users see or pass. The pipes are an internal implementation detail, that the user can't get at, so we should abstract them away from the user's interface.

I kind of like `input=b""` better than `stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL`. Obviously they're equivalent semantically, but when we show the signature in the docs, `input=b""` seems easier to understand. Anyone who understands `input` in general will be able to figure out what `input=b""` means. But if we do `stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL`, then people still have to learn `input`, and then also know about the subprocess `stdin` arg (which is very complicated!), *and then* know what this weird `DEVNULL` thing is.

This thing where there are 3 different modes stdin could be in, and then two of the modes have more configuration (and one of them is really another like 7 modes, and includes passthrough as a special case)...... wedging this into Python's argument passing model is extremely awkward.

I almost wonder if we should drop `input`, and make it so `stdin` can be a bytestring, or else any of the things the `subprocess` would accept (except `PIPE`):

```python
def run_process(cmd, *, check=True, stdin=b"", capture_stdout=False, capture_stderr=False, **kwargs):
    ...
```

If we're splitting up `capture_output` and using the words `stdout` and `stderr`, then replacing `input` with `stdin` is nicely consistent.

@wgtdkp I am not sure if we can use the `Remove` and `Add` model you are suggesting (basically replacing the existing service with the new `Service` being committed).

I think it is possible and valid for subtypes to be added or removed in seperate (follow up) SRP update messages for an existing service. In such a case, the `service` we parse from the new `message` and want to commit may not have the full list of previously registered sub-types (just the new ones being added/removed), so we cannot replace the existing one with new one being committed and need to combine/merge them.

------

_Some more info on the data model I am considering for the sub-types_:

The `Service` is mainly a representation of a `SRV` and `TXT` records we get for a "service instance name". We can have multiple `PTR` records all pointing to the same "service instance name" (basically different sub-types names all associated with same `SRV`/`TXT` info or the same `Service` object). 

So the data model I am considering for subtypes is to have a list of sub-type label `HeapString`s in the `Service` object (I am leaning towards using a linked-list for this though also considered a fixed-size array which may be easier to implement but adds a hard limit on number of sub-types). We also need to track the "update time" for each sub-type seperately since they be registered in different messages and at different times.  If you have any suggestions/thoughts on this, please let me know. Thanks. 😃

It is fairly common to collect arguments for a sub-function via the **scan_network, just as you did. I would however not document them there, but simply refer to the function they apply to. Another thing is that `allow_network_scan` really is an argument for the e.g. `discover` function, not for `network_scan`, so I would leave that as a normal argument. Also, this whole thing, with unpacking arguments into a function call from a dict, works just fine even though network scan has normal arguments, so I would definitely keep those, since they carry more information. So to sum up, something like this is what I would suggest, and the way I think that pattern is usually implemented:

```python

def discover(
        timeout=5, include_invisible=False, interface_addr=None,
        allow_network_scan=False, **network_scan_kwargs
):
    """Discover Sonos zones on the local network.

    Return a set of `SoCo` instances for each zone found.
    Include invisible zones (bridges and slave zones in stereo pairs if
    ``include_invisible`` is `True`. Will block for up to ``timeout`` seconds,
     after which return `None` if no zones found.

        timeout (int, optional): block for this many seconds, at most.
            Defaults to 5.
        include_invisible (bool, optional): include invisible zones in the

        interface_addr (str or None): Discovery operates by sending UDP
            multicast datagrams. ``interface_addr`` is a string (dotted
            quad) representation of the network interface address to use as
            the source of the datagrams (i.e. it is a value for
            `socket.IP_MULTICAST_IF <socket>`). If `None` or not specified,
            the system default interface for UDP multicast messages will be
            used. This is probably what you want to happen. Defaults to
            `None`.
        allow_network_scan (bool, optional): If normal discovery fails, fall
            back to a scan of the attached network(s) to detect Sonos
            devices.
        **network_scan_kwargs: Arguments for the

    Returns: set: a set of `SoCo` instances, one for each zone

    ...

    """

def by_name(name, allow_network_scan=False, **network_scan_kwargs):
    """Return a device by name.

    Args:
        name (str): The name of the device to return.
        allow_network_scan (bool, optional): If normal discovery fails, fall
            back to a scan of the attached network(s) to detect Sonos
            devices.
        **network_scan_kwargs: Arguments for the
            :function:`scan_network` function. See its docstring for
            documentation Returns: set: a set of `SoCo` instances, one
            for each zone found, or else `None`.

    Returns:
        :class:`~.SoCo`: The first device encountered among all zone with the
            given player name. If none are found `None` is returned.
    """
    devices = discover(
        allow_network_scan=allow_network_scan, **network_scan_kwargs
    )
    if devices is None:
        return None

    for device in devices:
        if device.player_name == name:
            return device
    return None

    """Scan all attached networks for Sonos devices.

    Scans the IPv4 network attached to each interface to check for network devices
    with port 1400 open. Check IPs in parallel threads for efficiency. Once the first
    Sonos device is found, stop checking and use that device to find the other
    devices. Returns a set of `SoCo` instances, or `None` if no Sonos devices are
    discovered.

    This function is intended for use when the usual discovery functions aren't
    working, perhaps due to multicast problems on the network to which the SoCo
    host is attached.

    Args:
        max_threads (int, optional): The maximum number of threads to use when
            scanning the network.
        scan_timeout (float, optional): The network timeout in seconds to use when
            checking each IP address for a Sonos device
        min_netmask (int, optional): The minimum number of netmask bits. Used to
                constrain the network search space.
        include_invisible (bool, optional): Whether to include invisible Sonos devices
            in the set of devices returned.

    Returns:
        set: A set of `SoCo` instances, one for each zone found, or else `None`.
    """

My understanding of Verilator is that `wait` semantics doesn't exists since the programming model assumes a single-thread driver code. After `eval()` is called, the state of circuit is always synchronized and you can just use `if` statement to test out inside a loop. Partially it is because Verilator is not an event-driven simulator, unlike commercial simulators. In my opinion, if there is no multi-threaded driver implementation in Verilator, we would never worry about `wait` semantics.

We are changing the namespace here to prevent additional break of user code if the queue is accessed through the translation unit border. In that case user needs to recompile the both translation units. If the queue is not accessed in different translation units then there is no any breaking change. In any case, the user code is already broken and should be recompiled.
In short, the idea of changed namespace is to prevent additional issues (not break partial recompilation use case but enforce the whole application recompilation).

The annotations applied to internal data fields are the concrete types returned by the yaml module's load function. I looked for public APIs involving sequence types, and came up with this:

- Manifest.get_projects() and Manifest.projects() return List[Project], but the "list" here is part of the documented API; these don't return a tuple or any other iterable or sequence, but a real list
- Manifest.as_dict() and Manifest.as_frozen_dict() return Dict, which is similarly part of the contract
- Similar comments apply to Project.listdir_at(): the return value should be a list
- Project.git()'s extra_args kwarg is an Iterable already, as is Manifest.get_projects() "project_ids" kwarg

So I think argument types are already permissive in terms of what they accept, but deliberately conservative in terms of what they return. That matches my intent for their behavior. Hopefully that's what you meant.

Messages that are sent to Thread devices are also passed to Host tun interface in PLAT_UDP implementation.
So I think we should not check for `.IsHostInterface())` in this place.

We have two kinds of UDP sockets:
1. UDP sockets not using Platform UDP (e.g. MLE, TMF)
  - For these UDP sockets, IsHostInterface() is generally set to false for sending and received UDP messages. 
1. UDP sockets using Platform UDP
  - For these UDP sockets, IsHostInterface() tells where should the message be sent and from which host interface the received message is from. For example, `IsHostInterface` is set to false if the receiving host interface is `wpan0`, and is set to true otherwise.

The main issue that I'm concerned with is that it is relatively easy to end up with a file that is marked with `filter=lfs` in gitattributes but is not actually using git-lfs:
- If you don't have git-lfs installed, then git will just ignore the lfs filter and will commit as a regular file. 
- If you have it installed, but you edit gitattributes directly instead of using `git lfs track` and you don't `git add --renormalize`, then the files are also committed as regular files.

As an aside - it would be great to have a pre-commit check that catches these cases, checking that all files that have `filter=lfs` in gitattributes are actually being tracked by git-lfs. If you know a good way of enforcing that, then it would be easier to switch this to something else based on `git check-attr`

We should take a couple of h and go over all of these at some point.
- `canonical_transcript` is annotated by VEP, and corresponds to the Canonical transcript status in ENSEMBL: typically the longes transcript around. In many cases this overlaps with, but can absolutely be distinct from primary_transcript - is also typically expressed as ENSEBL transcript.
- `primary_transcript` is the HGNC gene preferred transcript, which should be used for clinical reporting nomenclature unless there are pressing exceptions. Is expressed as a REFSEQ transcript. In general, when we export only one thing, I think this should actually be `primary_transcript`, and may have to be fetched off the `hgnc_gene` at more points, as it is not currently on the variant-stored gene_obj we use here.

Personally, I'd stick with the DAPNET name, though. Reason: In the end, "hampager" is more or less a reference to the target audience that is permitted to use this API and pager devices (ham radio operators and pager devices). DAPNET is however the  actual protocol that is used for the communication (decentralized amateur paging network).  And yes, both are being hosted by the same team.

But in the end, it's a question similar to e.g. using the Matrix messenger protocol with a third-party messenger client that is not provided with by the Matrix team itself. In this case, you'd also stick to e.g. ``matrix://`` and not the client software's name.

You can of course support both ``dapnet://`` and ``hampager://`` qualifiers - I don't see any issue with that. It might also make sense to capitalize the ``service_name`` variable's value but I'll leave that up to you.

```python
def worker_init_fn(worker_id, world_size, rank):
    info = torch.utils.data.get_worker_info()
    num_workers = info.num_workers
    datapipe = info.dataset
    datapipe.apply_sharding(world_size * worker_num, rank * worker_num + worker_id)

# Initialize DataLoader within each rank with above function
dl = DataLoader(dataset, worker_init_fn=functools.partial(worker_init_fn, world_size=world_size, rank=rank), ...)
```

The question I am left with in my mind is this: is it safe? IMHO, I am tempted to say that we should ONLY consider the DCS config to be authoritative.

My working mental model is that, for a configuration element like this, it would really only be safe to take the DCS as authoritative. In such a model, the local config is not considered authoritative for this element.

Consider the case where we have a replica which has been added to a cluster, but the initially given local config did not include the `static_primary` element which is present in the DCS. This could become problematic if we treat the local config as authoritative for this element. Instead, if we treat only the DCS as authoritative, then divergent local configuration is forced to conform to the DCS for this element, ensuring that safety considerations are upheld.

I do believe that this is probably why Patroni is currently implemented (irrespective of this PR) to abort startup if the DCS can not be contacted.

Thoughts? Also, thanks for the discussion and consideration.

I think we can use CRTP model can have members as well. 

`static_cast` between a base class and sub-class should always be safe and work correctly,  no matter if the base class has member variable or not (or if subclass has other base classes).  In `static_cast` the compiler will actually correctly handle the pointer conversion and may change the raw pointer address value when casting from base to sub class (e.g., if the base and sub-class are not aligned in memory, i.e. don't start from same address). The only requirement when casting from base to sub class is to be sure that the pointer to base class we have is indeed coming from a subclass instance, and I think this would be always the case in CRTP inheritance. `reinterpret_cast` on other hand won't be correct and/or safe.

>> "The _Atomic keyword also can also be used in the form _Atomic(T), where T is a type, as a type specifier equivalent to _Atomic T."
>
> So semantically, they seem equivalent.

I think there are two sides here:

- It works only in one direction. I.e. every pair of qualifier-type `_Atomic T` can be written as specifier-type `_Atomic(T)`. It is not possible to do it vice-versa, i.e. not every pair of specifier-type  `_Atomic(T)` can be written as qualifier-type `_Atomic T`.
- For the AST I believe it may be important to distinguish the pair type. For instance, one can make style suggestions in this case like "Rewrite it this way or that way".

> `_Atomic(<some decl>)`, you can return the node for `<some decl>` with the atomic qualifier added, and so on recursively. Would that work, without a special node?

I do not believe it can work, because of the following example:

```C
_Atomic(int *) a;
_Atomic(int) *b;
_Atomic int *c;
```

`a` is an atomic storing `int` pointers, while `b` and `c` are pointers to atomic `int`. In my opinion, for this very reason I cannot put `_Atomic` to something like `quals` attribute in case it is a specifier, as it clearly defines a type hierarchy. I believe the closest what you have in the code is the `enum` specifier.

> FWIW I think `_Alignas` is different, because its argument has semantic meaning beyond wrapping another type.

Analyzed a Child's state transition: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/52448427/88484550-fc6e6900-cfa1-11ea-9c3b-ae7c50da1252.png)

I believe that the filtered state actually does not matters that much, whether it's `Valid` only or `ValidOrRestoring` . What we need to do is two things:

- When the Child enters the filtered state (`Valid` or `ValidOrRestoring`), we need the check the current multicast addresses and schedule MLR.req if necessary.
- When the Child sends Address Registration TLV (by ChildUpdateRequest or ChildUpdateResponse) in the filtered state, we update the multicast addresses and schedule MLR.req if necessary. 

So, if we filter `Valid` state, then we need to update multicast addresses when a Child's state becomes `kStateValid`. Then, only update multicast addresses in `kStateValid` state. 

If we filter `ValidOrRestoring` state, then we need to update multicast addresses when a Child's state becomes `kStateValid` or `kStateChildUpdateRequest`. Then, update multicast addresses in these two states.  

With this analysis, maybe filtering `Valid` state is a better idea because we already have a clear entrance for a Child to become valid: `MleRouter::SetChildStateToValid` which calls `Signal(OT_NEIGHBOR_TABLE_EVENT_CHILD_ADDED, aChild);`. We can update the initial multicast addresses in these two `SetChildStateToValid` or `Signal`.

Given that we need information both at the `LogicalOperator` and `PhysicalOperator` level to perform this fusion, not sure what else we can do here other than:
1. Push the remaining requisite information into the physical `MapOperator` (constructor args, lift `target_block_size` out of the bundler, etc.).
2. Push the block transform function into the logical `AbstractMap` operator and do the fusion at the logical operator level.

Each have their complications/cons, e.g. for (1) we'd need to clear the constructor args at `op.start()` in order to keep us from needlessly hanging on to object references, and for (2) we'd be muddying the logical layer with an execution-level concept (block transformations).

We get away with being sloppy because OSX and Linux do the sensible thing and have utf-8 as the default, but Windows is stuck back in cp1252 land, and goes haywire.

@Dreamsorcerer many tools have TTY auto-detection and use plain text output in non-interactive invocations. OTOH, most CIs support rendering ANSI colored terminal output. GHA runners don't allocate a TTY for job invocations (some other CIs do this). This means that all tools that don't special-case the CIs (which can be detected via env vars), don't produce ANSI output to be on the safe side. A lot of these apps have a CLI flag to force the color output anyway. And there's a conventional env var that is gaining popularity over the past years for doing the same — `$FORCE_COLOR`. If it is set, it is expected that a program supporting it would produce colorized output regardless of the presence of a TTY. Its popular counterpart `$NO_COLOR` even has its own website — https://no-color.org. Also, some software that doesn't yet support `FORCE_COLOR` (like old MyPy), has own env vars (e.g. `MYPY_FORCE_COLOR`).

As you can see, there's a number of reasons you haven't seen the colored output, especially if you're mostly checking out the CI logs.
Here's what I've been recently integrating into CIs, having realized that most of my pytest and mypy runs didn't produce fancy logs: [jaraco/skeleton@`e22ae08` (#67)](https://github.com/jaraco/skeleton/pull/67/commits/e22ae083f7e1ab41dfa8bb81bc7027281ad9dae6#diff-7829468e86c1cc5d5133195b5cb48e1ff6c75e3e9203777f6b2e379d9e4882b3R14-R17).

Back to the `color_output` setting — I'm going to guess it may only default to `true` in certain envs, but not the others. So adding it would still be warranted. Alternatively (or additionally), it'd be nice to have that patch I linked above integrated too.

Not sure about `error_summary` but sometimes I just follow the principle of explicit being better than implicit. From the PoV of a config reader, this reduces the mental overhead of having to figure out what the other settings are present implicitly. Plus, this saves from surprise defaults changes on updates.

Yes, a simple plain lock<sup>*)<sup>.

<sup>*)</sup> The lock is actually a noop lock in the async implementation, because there is not `await` while the lock is acquired: this means there is no possibility of concurrency given that Python's async implements cooperative concurrency. I.e., you never get interrupted, unless you yield to the event loop (some `await` calls).

I've compared these two approaches and it appeared that the proposed one almost always faster:

<details><summary>Micro-benchmark and its results</summary>

```python
import modin.pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import timeit


def is_dtype_to_pick(dtype):
    return dtype == "float"


def fn1(df):
    to_convert = {}
    fallback = False
    for col, dt in df.dtypes.items():
        if is_dtype_to_pick(dt):
            if df[col].hasnans:
                fallback = True
                break
            else:
                to_convert[col] = dt


def fn2(df):
    cols_to_select = [col for col, dt in df.dtypes.items() if is_dtype_to_pick(dt)]
    if len(cols_to_select) == 0:
        return
    selected_df = df[cols_to_select]
    fallback = selected_df.isna().any().any()
    if not fallback:
        to_convert = selected_df.dtypes.items()


NROWS = 1_000_000
NCOLS = 10

df_no_floats = pd.DataFrame({f"col{i}": np.arange(NROWS) for i in range(NCOLS)})
df_has_half_float_no_nans = df_no_floats.astype({col: "float" for col in df_no_floats.columns[:len(df_no_floats.columns) // 2]})

df_has_half_float_nans_first = df_has_half_float_no_nans.copy()
df_has_half_float_nans_first.loc[0, df_has_half_float_nans_first.select_dtypes("float").columns[0]] = np.nan

df_has_half_float_nans_last = df_has_half_float_no_nans.copy()
df_has_half_float_nans_first.loc[0, df_has_half_float_nans_first.select_dtypes("float").columns[-1]] = np.nan

NRUNS = 5

print("df_no_float:")
print("for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn1(df_no_floats), number=NRUNS))
print("non-for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn2(df_no_floats), number=NRUNS))

print("\ndf_has_half_float_no_nans:")
print("for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn1(df_has_half_float_no_nans), number=NRUNS))
print("non-for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn2(df_has_half_float_no_nans), number=NRUNS))

print("\ndf_has_half_float_nans_first:")
print("for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn1(df_has_half_float_nans_first), number=NRUNS))
print("non-for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn2(df_has_half_float_nans_first), number=NRUNS))

print("\ndf_has_half_float_nans_last:")
print("for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn1(df_has_half_float_nans_last), number=NRUNS))
print("non-for-loop:", timeit.timeit(lambda: fn2(df_has_half_float_nans_last), number=NRUNS))

```

> Are they necessarily defaults, or the actual resolved values?

I added the `_defaults` suffix at the last minute here. In most cases, these are the actual values. The exception is the `AWS::S3::ForcePathStyle` builtin. To maintain full backwards compatibility, this will sometimes need to be changed from the default value based on operation-specific operation. 

Here is a specific example:

```python
import botocore.session
from botocore.config import Config

session = botocore.session.get_session()
client = session.create_client(
    's3',
    config=Config(s3={addressing_style='path'}),
)

client.get_object_attributes(
    Bucket="arn:aws:s3:us-west-2:1234567890:accesspoint/my-bucket-access-point",
    Key="object.txt",
    ObjectAttributes=["ETag", "Checksum"],
)
```

If we set `AWS::S3::ForcePathStyle = True` purely based on the value of `addressing_style` in the config, then the ruleset will return this error:

```
Path-style addressing cannot be used with ARN buckets
```

Botocore's current behavior is to silently ignore the `addressing_style` config when an ARN is used. To maintain this behavior, we need to change the `AWS::S3::ForcePathStyle` to `False` when the bucket name is an ARN. The bucket name can be different for each operation. This is why I decided to call the builtin values assigned at client construction time "defaults". `AWS::S3::ForcePathStyle` is the only builtin where operation-time modifications are necessary, at least for now.

import torch

class ImageNetModel:
    def __init__(self):
        self.model = resnet50(pretrained=True).eval().half().cuda()

    def __call__(self, input_tensor_np):
        input_tensor = torch.from_numpy(input_tensor_np).half().cuda()
        with torch.no_grad():
            output_tensor = self.model(input_tensor)
            result = torch.argmax(output_tensor, dim=1).cpu()
        return result.numpy()
```

Cached executor idle timeout differs for different use-cases.
Jupyter notebook usage tends for longer time-gaps between two executive actions. 
It is better not clean cache for longer times
We have seen that increasing cached timeout for jupyter notebooks has helped in running their jobs faster without recomputation.

* Have a private api to identify whether a job is coming from Jupyterhub framework or not.
* Even better having an api which sets spark configs for Jupyterhub framework use-cases (e.g. min executors as 0, timeout as 45min+), log cost for jupyterhub notebooks

* Set 45 min or higher cached timeout for jupyterhub jobs (with message of timeout value being set)

async def test_read_timeout_on_write(aiohttp_client: Any) -> None:
    async def gen_payload() -> AsyncIterator[str]:
        # Delay writing to ensure read timeout isn't triggered before writing completes.
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        yield b"foo"

    async def handler(request: web.Request) -> web.Response:
        return web.Response(body=await request.read())

    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_put("/", handler)

    timeout = aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=None, sock_read=0.1)
    client = await aiohttp_client(app)
    async with client.put("/", data=gen_payload(), timeout=timeout) as resp:
        result = await resp.read()  # Should not trigger a read timeout.
    assert result == b"foo"
```

> but the problem is the way that lightgbm splits on categorical features is not deterministic

This is not true. It's possible to get `lightgbm` to deterministically produce the same model given the same input data... it's just not deterministic *by default*.

You can use the following code (pay special attention to the values of `params`) as an example.

```python
import lightgbm as lgb
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)

X_df = pd.DataFrame()
rnd = np.random.RandomState(0)
n_cat_values = rnd.randint(2, 10, size=X.shape[1])
for i in range(X.shape[1]):
    bins = np.linspace(0, 1, num=n_cat_values[i]+1)
    X_df[f"cat_col_{i}"] = pd.qcut(X[:, i], q=bins, labels=range(n_cat_values[i])).as_unordered()

params = {
    "n_estimators": 10,
    "num_leaves": 3,
    "min_data_in_bin": 1,
    "force_col_wise": True,
    "deterministic": True,
    "num_threads": 1,
    "seed": 708,
    "verbose": -1
}

gbm1 = lgb.LGBMClassifier(**params)
gbm1.fit(X_df, y)
gbm1_df = gbm1.booster_.trees_to_dataframe()

# prove that in 100 repeated training runs, the exact same model is produced
for i in range(100):
    print(f"iteration: {i}")
    gbm = lgb.LGBMClassifier(**params).fit(X_df, y)
    pd.testing.assert_frame_equal(gbm1_df, gbm.booster_.trees_to_dataframe())
```

Try modifying your tests to pass similar values of `params`, to produce the same model every time the tests are run.

```

Not quite follow here -- let me organize the flows for these two options:
- Turn on ONNX_DISABLE_STATIC_REGISTRATION: runtime might register onnx schema for certain opset_version multiple times
  - Initially loaded_schema_version=-1; which means no schema has been registered yet
  - runtime will further call `RegisterOnnxOperatorSetSchema` with certain opset_version to register specified opset_version (and `loaded_schema_version` will be set as that specified version).
  - (Optional) runtime may call `DeregisterOnnxOperatorSetSchema` to clear all registration (and `loaded_schema_version` will be set as -1) and then call `RegisterOnnxOperatorSetSchema` with another opset_version  (and `loaded_schema_version` will be changed accordingly).
- Turn off ONNX_DISABLE_STATIC_REGISTRATION: runtime will just register onnx schema once
  - Initially loaded_schema_version=-1; which means no schema has been registered yet
  - runtime will further call `RegisterOnnxOperatorSetSchema` to register all onnx schema and then `loaded_schema_version` will be set as 0.

Thus, I think anyway OpSchemaRegistry::loaded_schema_version will initially declared as -1 by default, which lets onnxruntime know there is no schema has been registered yet. Did I missing something obvious?

Thanks for the note. Here is that `_chain_future`:

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/61762b93871419b34f02d83cee5ca0d94d4a2903/Lib/asyncio/futures.py#L365-L406

what it basically does regarding cancellation is that it attaches `loop.call_soon_threadsafe(fut2.cancel)` to `fut1` done callback if fut1 is cancelled.

Maybe I'm missing something but I'm not sure how it helps us here. Here we need to initiate such cancellation, not to propagate a cancellation from another future. Initiating cancellation is easy - just do `loop.call_soon_threadsafe(executor.cancel)` - and it is already part of that code (see line 450). But we also want to wait for the result of that cancel and to return that result to the original caller. That's why semaphore is added for such wait/wakeup. Yes, `call_soon_threadsafe` can be used from any thread, but if run originally on the loop thread, initiate the semaphore, invoke loop.call_soon_threadsafe and then initiate waiting on `sema.acquire`, the loop thread will block without having a chance to execute what was scheduled. In other words it will turn out as deadlock even if calling just `call_soon_threadsafe` is ok from any thread.

Once again maybe I'm missing something. If it is indeed the case could you please suggest how you would implement this part in a simpler way?

Ah I see what you are saying! Tracing can be thought of as a different process than metrics (where metrics would be more similar to gathering information outputted by Ray).  You can think of tracing as simply wrapping different events and gathering all the necessary information of timestamps, event names, etc. automatically. So if you wrap a code sample with `with tracer.start_as_current_span("foo"):`, the name of the span would be `foo` and it would automatically track the timestamps and such! Does that make sense? Rather than collecting events after they have occurred, tracing is gathering the information *as the events occur.*

Nit: the disconnect between the description and the rule purpose gets to the fact that when you "set up a lockfile", what you're actually doing is "setting up a resolve", and the lockfile is the on-disk representation of the resolve. When someone wants to consume the resolve, they'll need to make a request with a very similar shape.

Moving forward, I expect the terminology will move in the direction of "requesting a resolve" (with an optional lockfile location field), but it's possible it should affect the API sooner.

Not all redis clients declare the `connection_pool` field. In aioredis v1.X.X we instrument [Redis.execute](https://github.com/aio-libs/aioredis-py/blob/v1.3.1/aioredis/commands/__init__.py#L31) and in v2.X.X we instrument [StrictRedis.execute_command](https://github.com/aio-libs/aioredis-py/blob/6612ba716320c43a87b2b6c159a62e8569bda86c/aioredis/client.py#L630). The v1 Redis class does not have a connection_pool attribute while v2 has this field

We already have a `hasattr(instance, "connection_pool")` check on redis instances in [flask_cache/utils.py](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/master/ddtrace/contrib/flask_cache/utils.py#L45) so I there should be other redis libraries that do not declare a `connection_pool` field.
